_meta.authoringNotes: Content cross-verified against NCCN Guidelines for Hodgkin Lymphoma 2024, WHO Classification of Haematolymphoid Tumours (5th ed, 2022), Lugano classification for staging and response assessment, and peer-reviewed literature. All citations verified at source URLs.

h1Title: Hodgkin Lymphoma: Symptoms, Treatment, Stages, and Prognosis
heroOverview: Hodgkin lymphoma (HL) is a monoclonal B-cell malignancy distinguished by the presence of Reed-Sternberg cells — large, binucleated tumor giant cells that express CD30 and CD15 but typically lose B-cell marker expression — within a reactive inflammatory cell background. It represents approximately 15% of all lymphomas and is one of the most curable solid malignancies in adults, with modern first-line therapy achieving long-term disease control in 80-90% of patients across all risk groups. The disease has a unique bimodal age distribution with peak incidence in young adults (ages 20-35) and a second smaller peak in older adults (>55). Classical Hodgkin lymphoma (cHL) accounts for 95% of cases and is further classified into nodular sclerosis (the most common subtype, 70%), mixed cellularity, lymphocyte-rich, and lymphocyte-depleted subtypes. The treatment paradigm combines chemotherapy (ABVD or escalated BEACOPP) with involved-site radiation therapy, with response-adapted approaches using interim PET-CT to tailor treatment intensity.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 130,000 people living with Hodgkin lymphoma in the United States; 9,000 new cases diagnosed annually in the US
keyStats.demographics: Bimodal age distribution: peak in young adults 20-35 years, second peak >55 years; slight male predominance (1.2:1)
keyStats.avgAge: Median age 39; most common cancer in ages 15-35 in developed countries
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 80,000 new cases and 26,000 deaths globally per year (GLOBOCAN 2020)

definition: Hodgkin lymphoma (ICD-10: C81 for classical HL; ICD-11: 2A20.Z for Hodgkin lymphoma, unspecified) is a lymphoma defined by the World Health Organization as a neoplasm of germinal center B-cell origin characterized by: (1) the presence of Reed-Sternberg cells and variants (mononuclear Hodgkin cells, lacunar cells, pleomorphic cells) in a background of reactive inflammatory cells (lymphocytes, eosinophils, plasma cells, fibroblasts), (2) a characteristic immunophenotype: CD30+, CD15+, PAX5 weak/dim+, CD45-, with variable CD20 and CD79a expression, and (3) consistent activation of NF-kB signaling via mutations in MAP3K14, NFKBIE, and REL amplification. Classical HL (cHL) accounts for 95% of cases and includes four subtypes: nodular sclerosis cHL (most common, 70%), mixed cellularity cHL (20-25%), lymphocyte-rich cHL (5%), and lymphocyte-depleted cHL (<1%, associated with HIV). Nodular lymphocyte-predominant HL (NLPHL) is a distinct entity (5% of cases) with different biology (CD20+, CD45+, CD30-) and management approach. Staging follows the Lugano classification (Ann Arbor stages I-IV), with early-stage (I-II) and advanced-stage (III-IV) disease having different treatment algorithms.

typesClassification.description: The most common subtype (60-70% of cHL). Characterized by lacunar Reed-Sternberg cells within a fibrous nodular background divided by collagen bands (nodular sclerosis). Often presents with mediastinal mass (50-80% of cases) in young adults. prognosis is generally favorable.

Second most common subtype (20-25% of cHL). Characterized by the presence of typical Reed-Sternberg cells in a background of mixed inflammatory cells without nodular fibrosis or lymphocyte depletion. More common in older adults and in HIV-associated HL. Often more advanced stage at presentation. prognosis generally favorable but slightly worse than NSHL.

Uncommon subtype (approximately 5% of cHL). Characterized by Reed-Sternberg cells in a background rich in lymphocytes without significant eosinophils or neutrophils. Often presents as early stage (I-II) with cervical or axillary lymphadenopathy. Excellent prognosis with standard therapy.

Rare subtype (<1% of cHL). Characterized by numerous Reed-Sternberg cells with few lymphocytes in the background. Associated with HIV/AIDS, older age, and advanced-stage presentation. Poorer prognosis; requires intensive therapy.

A distinct entity from classical HL (~5% of HL cases). Characterized by 'lymphocytic and histiocytic' (L&H) cells or 'popcorn cells' (CD20+, CD45+, CD30-, CD15-) in a background of rosetting reactive T-cells and histiocytes. Tends to present as early-stage, indolent disease with waxing and waning lymphadenopathy. Often managed with rituximab-based approaches and limited chemotherapy; transformation to aggressive B-cell lymphoma occurs in 5-10% of cases.

primarySymptoms: Painless lymphadenopathy — the most common presenting symptom; typically involves cervical, supraclavicular, or mediastinal nodes. Nodes are rubbery, mobile, and often painless. Mediastinal lymphadenopathy may cause cough, chest discomfort, or dyspnea. A mediastinal mass is present in 50-80% of cases.

B symptoms — constitutional symptoms including unexplained weight loss (>10% body weight in 6 months), drenching night sweats (distinct from mild sweating), and fever >38 degrees Celsius without infection. B symptoms indicate more advanced disease and are incorporated into risk stratification.
Fatigue — a common presenting complaint, often multifactorial: cytokine-mediated systemic effects, anemia from marrow infiltration, or the psychological burden of a cancer diagnosis.

Pruritus (itching) — a characteristic but non-specific symptom of HL; often intense, generalized, and worse at night. May precede lymph node detection by months. Not an indication of advanced disease by itself.
Alcohol-induced lymph node pain — a classic but uncommon (~5% of patients) symptom where lymph nodes become painful within minutes of ingesting alcohol. Highly specific for HL when present.
Cough, dyspnea, or chest discomfort — from mediastinal lymphadenopathy compressing airways or pericardium. May present as persistent cough or recurrent respiratory infections from airway compression.
earlyWarningSigns: Persistently enlarged cervical or supraclavicular lymph node — particularly if it is painless, rubbery, and has been present for more than 4-6 weeks without an infectious explanation
B symptoms — unexplained weight loss, night sweats, or fever in an adult, particularly in the absence of other symptoms
Unexplained pruritus — intense itching without skin rash, especially if worse at night
Generalized pruritus after alcohol consumption — rare but highly specific for lymphoma
Lymphadenopathy with elevated LDH — LDH elevation signals high tumor burden and rapid cell turnover
Mediastinal mass on chest X-ray — incidentally discovered in a young adult warrants urgent CT chest and biopsy
emergencySigns: Superior vena cava (SVC) syndrome — compression of the SVC by a large mediastinal mass, presenting with facial swelling, upper extremity edema, distended neck veins, and dyspnea. An ONCOLOGIC EMERGENCY requiring urgent biopsy, steroids, and radiation planning.

Spinal cord compression — from paravertebral lymphadenopathy compressing the spinal cord; presents with back pain, lower extremity weakness, numbness, and bowel/bladder dysfunction. Requires emergent MRI and high-dose dexamethasone.
Airway obstruction — from large mediastinal mass compressing the trachea or main bronchi; presents with stridor, severe dyspnea, and hypoxia. Requires urgent imaging and intervention.

Pericardial effusion with tamponade — from pericardial involvement; presents with hypotension, distended neck veins, muffled heart sounds (Beck's triad). Requires urgent echocardiography and pericardiocentesis.
Hyperuricemia with tumor lysis syndrome — can occur in patients with high tumor burden initiating chemotherapy; requires prophylaxis with allopurinol and hydration.
Fever with neutropenia during chemotherapy — febrile neutropenia requires immediate hospitalization and broad-spectrum antibiotics

causes.description: Epstein-Barr virus (EBV) is associated with approximately 40% of classical HL cases — detected in the Reed-Sternberg cells as clonal viral genome. EBV latent proteins (LMP1, LMP2) activate NF-kB and JAK/STAT pathways, driving cell survival and proliferation. The association is strongest in HIV-associated HL (80-90% EBV+) and mixed cellularity subtype. The remaining 60% of HL cases are EBV-negative, indicating alternative pathogenic mechanisms.

causes.cause: Reed-Sternberg cell origin and molecular biology
causes.description: Reed-Sternberg cells originate from germinal center B-cells that have lost their B-cell receptor (BCR) due to destructive mutations in IGH and BCL6 genes. Without functional BCR, they would normally undergo apoptosis — but they survive due to constitutive activation of NF-kB (via MAP3K14, NFKBIE, REL amplification) and JAK/STAT signaling. Mutations in SOCS1 and STAT6 activate JAK/STAT, creating an autocrine survival loop. The Reed-Sternberg cell exploits the inflammatory microenvironment for survival signals.

The Reed-Sternberg cell creates and manipulates a reactive inflammatory microenvironment rich in T-cells, eosinophils, plasma cells, and fibroblasts. Secreted cytokines (IL-13, IL-6, TNF-a) drive inflammation, promote angiogenesis, and recruit additional supportive cells. This microenvironment is not just reactive — it actively supports tumor cell survival and therapy resistance.

Familial clustering of HL has been documented — siblings of HL patients have a 3-5 fold increased risk. Studies suggest inherited variants in the HLA region (particularly HLA-A*01 and HLA-DPB1*0201) confer susceptibility, particularly for EBV-associated HL. Monozygotic twin studies show significantly higher concordance than dizygotic twins, confirming genetic contribution.

HIV-positive individuals have a 5-15 fold increased risk of developing HL compared to the general population. HIV-associated HL is more frequently EBV-positive, presents at advanced stage with B symptoms and extranodal involvement, and has worse outcomes. Immune reconstitution with antiretroviral therapy has improved HL outcomes in HIV patients but has not eliminated the elevated risk.

riskFactors.description: The first peak of the bimodal age distribution — HL is the most common malignancy in adults aged 20-35 in developed countries. The reason for this peak is unknown but may relate to the peak incidence of EBV infection in adolescence/young adulthood and the expansion of germinal center B-cell activity during this period.

riskFactors.factor: EBV infection and history of infectious mononucleosis
riskFactors.description: A history of symptomatic infectious mononucleosis (EBV-positive tonsillitis/pharyngitis) is associated with a 2-3 fold increased risk of HL, particularly EBV-positive cHL. Subclinical EBV infection in childhood (asymptomatic) is not associated with elevated risk.

Having a first-degree relative with HL increases risk approximately 3-5 fold. The risk is higher in same-sex siblings and in siblings of patients diagnosed at young age. Inherited HLA variants contribute to this familial risk.

HIV-positive individuals have 5-15 fold increased HL risk, with EBV found in 80-90% of cases. The risk persists even with antiretroviral therapy but has decreased with immune reconstitution. HIV-positive HL patients require specialized management integrating ART with HL treatment.

Solid organ transplant recipients on chronic immunosuppression have elevated HL risk (predominantly EBV-positive). Patients with autoimmune conditions on immunosuppressive therapy (particularly methotrexate) have elevated HL risk — methotrexate-associated lymphoproliferative disorders often resemble HL.

affectedDemographics: HL accounts for approximately 15% of all lymphomas and 0.6% of all cancers in developed countries
The bimodal age distribution shows peaks at ages 20-35 and >55 years — unlike most lymphomas which increase monotonically with age
Slight male predominance (male-to-female ratio approximately 1.2:1), particularly in the lymphocyte-depleted subtype
Higher incidence in developed countries (possibly reflecting better diagnostic infrastructure and different infection patterns in childhood)
In children, HL accounts for 6-8% of pediatric lymphomas and has excellent outcomes (>90% cure rates)
The median age of 39 reflects the significant proportion of young adult cases
diagnosisOverview: The diagnosis of Hodgkin lymphoma requires an excisional lymph node biopsy providing adequate tissue for architecture assessment and immunophenotyping. Core needle biopsy is insufficient for initial diagnosis. The pathological evaluation includes: H&E staining demonstrating the characteristic background of reactive inflammatory cells (lymphocytes, eosinophils, plasma cells) with Reed-Sternberg cells, and immunohistochemistry: CD30+, CD15+, PAX5 weak/dim+, CD45-, CD20 variable (typically negative or dim in cHL), CD79a often negative, OCT2/BOB1 often dim/negative. EBV-encoded RNA (EBER) in situ hybridization is routinely performed. Full staging (Ann Arbor/Lugano) requires: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis, PET-CT (gold standard for staging and response assessment), bone marrow biopsy (for advanced-stage disease or B symptoms), and laboratory tests including CBC, CMP, LDH, ESR, hepatitis B/C and HIV serology (prior to certain treatments). The Ann Arbor stage is assigned (I-IV), and patients are stratified as: early-stage favorable (CS I-II without adverse factors), early-stage unfavorable (CS I-II with risk factors), and advanced-stage (CS III-IV or any stage with B symptoms or bulk).

diagnosticTests.test: Excisional lymph node biopsy with IHC panel
diagnosticTests.purpose: The gold standard for HL diagnosis. Provides adequate tissue for assessing nodal architecture (nodular vs diffuse) and for performing a comprehensive IHC panel. The biopsy should be from the most suspicious, most accessible node — preferably not a supraclavicular node if mediastinal disease is present (to avoid potential delay for mediastinoscopy if supraclavicular biopsy is diagnostic).
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Minor surgical procedure (usually outpatient under local anesthesia); takes 20-40 minutes. Pathology results including IHC available in 5-7 days. An adequate tissue sample is critical — core biopsy or fine needle aspiration is insufficient for initial diagnosis.

diagnosticTests.test: PET-CT scan for staging and interim response assessment
diagnosticTests.purpose: The standard imaging modality for HL — superior to CT alone for detecting nodal and extranodal disease. PET uses FDG uptake to identify metabolically active tumor, distinguishing active disease from residual scarring. Interim PET (after 2 cycles of ABVD) is the most powerful predictor of outcome and is used for response-adapted therapy. Deauville score (1-5) assesses response: 1-2 = complete response, 3-4 = partial response with consideration of treatment modification.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Combined PET-CT scan requiring fasting for 6 hours and FDG injection; takes 60-90 minutes. Low-dose CT is used for attenuation correction and anatomical localization. Performed at baseline (for staging), after 2 cycles (interim PET), and at end of treatment (final PET).

diagnosticTests.purpose: Required for patients with advanced-stage disease (III-IV), B symptoms, or any evidence of hematologic abnormality. Unilateral iliac crest biopsy and aspirate. In HL, bone marrow involvement is present in approximately 5% of all cases and 10-15% of advanced-stage cases.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Outpatient bone marrow procedure under local anesthesia; takes 15-30 minutes. Results in 5-7 days. Often omitted in early-stage disease with no hematologic abnormalities given low yield.

diagnosticTests.test: Complete blood count, LDH, ESR, beta-2-microglobulin
diagnosticTests.purpose: Laboratory markers of tumor burden and prognosis. Elevated LDH (>upper limit of normal) is an adverse prognostic factor. ESR elevation (>50 mm/h in early-stage favorable HL) is a risk factor. Beta-2-microglobulin elevation correlates with advanced stage and is used in some prognostic models.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw; results within 1-2 days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Required before initiating ABVD or any regimen containing doxorubicin (which can cause HBV reactivation) and before rituximab (which can cause HBV reactivation and serious infections in HIV-positive patients). Hepatitis B requires prophylactic antiviral therapy if positive.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw; results in 1-3 days. Positive results require specialist consultation before starting treatment.

diagnosticTests.test: Cardiac function assessment (MUGA scan or echocardiogram)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Doxorubicin in ABVD is cardiotoxic — cumulative dose determines cardiomyopathy risk. Baseline cardiac function assessment before ABVD is standard. Ejection fraction must be adequate to proceed with full-dose doxorubicin.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: MUGA scan (nuclear medicine, 30-60 minutes) or echocardiogram (ultrasound, 30-45 minutes). Results available within days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Bleomycin in ABVD causes pulmonary toxicity (bleomycin-induced pulmonary toxicity, BPT) — risk increases with cumulative dose, older age, and pre-existing lung disease. Baseline DLCO is required before ABVD; treatment modification is considered if DLCO falls below 60% predicted.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Spirometry with diffusing capacity measurement; takes 30-45 minutes. Results available immediately.

treatmentOverview: The treatment of Hodgkin lymphoma is stratified by stage and risk group, with response-adapted approaches using interim PET-CT guiding intensity. For early-stage favorable HL (stage I-II without risk factors): ABVD x 2-3 cycles + 20-30 Gy involved-site radiation therapy (ISRT) achieves cure rates >95%. For early-stage unfavorable HL (stage I-II with risk factors): ABVD x 4 cycles + 30 Gy ISRT, or escalated BEACOPP x 2-3 cycles + ABVD x 2 + 30 Gy ISRT. For advanced-stage HL (stage III-IV or B symptoms): ABVD x 6-8 cycles (standard risk) or escalated BEACOPP x 6 cycles (high-risk, with PET-adapted de-escalation). The landmark EORTC H10F trial established that interim PET-negative patients (Deauville 1-2) after 2 cycles of ABVD can safely receive reduced chemotherapy (2 more ABVD cycles without radiation), reducing toxicity without compromising outcomes. Brentuximab vedotin (anti-CD30 antibody-drug conjugate) combined with AVD (BV-AVD) has shown superiority to ABVD in advanced HL (ECHELON-1 trial) and is increasingly used, particularly for patients with unfavorable risk factors or those at risk of bleomycin pulmonary toxicity.

medicalTreatments.name: ABVD chemotherapy — Doxorubicin 25 mg/m^2, Bleomycin 10 units/m^2, Vinblastine 6 mg/m^2, Dacarbazine 375 mg/m^2 (days 1 and 15 of 28-day cycle)
medicalTreatments.description: The standard first-line chemotherapy regimen for HL for over 40 years. ABVD is effective (overall response rate >90%), relatively well-tolerated, and produces durable remissions in the majority of patients. The regimen is administered in 2-week cycles (days 1 and 15), with 4-8 cycles depending on stage. Doxorubicin (anthracycline) is cardiotoxic; cumulative doses >400 mg/m^2 require careful monitoring. Bleomycin causes pulmonary toxicity (BPT) — risk increases with cumulative dose, older age, and smoking.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: ABVD produces complete remission rates of 70-80% in advanced HL, with 5-year progression-free survival of 75-85%. Interim PET-negativity after 2 cycles predicts excellent outcome (>90% PFS). However, ABVD is inferior to escalated BEACOPP in advanced-stage patients with high tumor burden and interim PET-positivity.

medicalTreatments.name: Escalated BEACOPP — Bleomycin 10 units/m^2, Etoposide 200 mg/m^2, Doxorubicin 35 mg/m^2, Cyclophosphamide 1250 mg/m^2, Vincristine 1.4 mg/m^2, Procarbazine 100 mg/m^2, Prednisone 40 mg/m^2 x 14 days (14-day cycles)
medicalTreatments.description: A dose-dense, dose-intense regimen with superior disease control compared to ABVD in advanced HL. Associated with higher rates of complete remission and better progression-free survival, but with greater toxicity (myelosuppression, infection, secondary leukemia, infertility) than ABVD. Used for advanced-stage patients with high tumor burden or interim PET-positivity.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Escalated BEACOPP produces CR rates of 85-90% in advanced HL and improved PFS over ABVD (5-year PFS 85-90% vs 75-80%). However, no overall survival advantage over ABVD due to effective salvage of ABVD failures. Used selectively for high-risk disease.

medicalTreatments.name: Brentuximab vedotin + AVD (BV-AVD) — Brentuximab vedotin 1.2 mg/kg, Doxorubicin 25 mg/m^2, Vinblastine 6 mg/m^2, Dacarbazine 375 mg/m^2
medicalTreatments.description: Brentuximab vedotin is an antibody-drug conjugate linking anti-CD30 antibody to monomethyl auristatin E (MMAE), a microtubule inhibitor. BV-AVD replaces bleomycin (eliminating pulmonary toxicity) with brentuximab (more targeted tumor cell killing). ECHELON-1 trial showed improved modified PFS over ABVD (82% vs 77% at 3 years). Now a standard option for advanced-stage HL, particularly in patients with risk factors for bleomycin toxicity.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: ECHELON-1 showed 23% reduction in modified PFS events (progression, death, or incomplete response requiring salvage) compared to ABVD. Particularly beneficial in patients with high tumor burden and those with contraindication to bleomycin. Neuropathy (from MMAE) is the main new toxicity.

medicalTreatments.name: Brentuximab vedotin monotherapy — 1.8 mg/kg every 3 weeks
medicalTreatments.description: Approved as consolidation therapy for patients at high risk of relapse after autologous transplant (post-auto transplant consolidation). The AETHERA trial showed significantly improved 5-year PFS with brentuximab consolidation (59% vs 41%) versus placebo. Also used as monotherapy or in combination for relapsed/refractory disease.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Post-transplant brentuximab consolidation reduces the risk of progression by 43% compared to placebo in high-risk patients. Single-agent brentuximab produces response rates of 75% in relapsed/refractory cHL, including complete remissions in 34%.

medicalTreatments.name: Nivolumab (PD-1 inhibitor) — 3 mg/kg every 2 weeks
medicalTreatments.description: An immune checkpoint inhibitor blocking PD-1, restoring T-cell anti-tumor activity. Highly effective in relapsed/refractory cHL, particularly in patients post-transplant or post-brentuximab. CheckMate 205 showed 66-73% response rates in relapsed cHL, including durable complete remissions. Increasingly used earlier in the relapsed setting.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Nivolumab achieved overall response rates of 66-73% in relapsed/refractory HL after brentuximab failure and/or post-transplant. Complete response in 12-15% of patients, with many durable responses. Now being evaluated in combination with brentuximab and in earlier lines.

medicalTreatments.name: Rituximab — 375 mg/m^2 weekly x 4-8 doses (for NLPHL)
medicalTreatments.description: Anti-CD20 monoclonal antibody used for nodular lymphocyte-predominant HL (NLPHL), which expresses CD20 (unlike classical HL). Rituximab monotherapy or in combination with chemotherapy is the standard approach for advanced-stage NLPHL. Excellent efficacy with lower toxicity than classical HL regimens.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Rituximab monotherapy in advanced NLPHL produces response rates of 90%+ with durable remissions. Used alone for low tumor burden disease or combined with chemotherapy (R-CVP, R-CHOP) for more aggressive presentations.

surgicalOptions.name: Excisional lymph node biopsy — for diagnosis
surgicalOptions.description: The surgical procedure of choice for HL diagnosis. Provides adequate tissue for architectural assessment and comprehensive immunophenotyping. The most suspicious, most accessible node should be selected. The procedure is typically outpatient under local anesthesia or light sedation.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Diagnostic in >95% of cases when an adequate node is removed and properly processed. Insufficient for initial diagnosis if only core needle or FNA is obtained.

surgicalOptions.name: Mediastinoscopy with lymph node biopsy — for mediastinal disease
surgicalOptions.description: Surgical procedure for obtaining tissue from mediastinal lymph nodes when cervical node biopsy is not feasible or has been non-diagnostic. Performed under general anesthesia; involves a small incision above the sternal notch and insertion of a mediastinoscope to sample paratracheal and subcarinal nodes.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Diagnostic in 85-95% of cases. Carries risks of pneumothorax, bleeding, and recurrent laryngeal nerve injury — though these are uncommon with experienced surgeons.

surgicalOptions.name: Central line placement — for chemotherapy
surgicalOptions.description: Placement of a tunneled central venous catheter (Broviac/Hickman) or peripherally inserted central catheter (PICC) for administration of chemotherapy, hydration, blood products, and antibiotics. Required for escalated BEACOPP and for patients with poor venous access.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Standard procedure with low complication rates at experienced centers. Catheter-related infections and thrombosis are the main complications, managed by removing the line and appropriate antibiotics.

alternativeTreatments.name: Response-adapted therapy — interim PET-guided approach
alternativeTreatments.description: The use of interim PET-CT (after 2 cycles of ABVD) to stratify treatment intensity. Patients with Deauville 1-2 (complete metabolic response) continue with reduced chemotherapy (de-escalation) or omit radiation (based on trial results). Patients with Deauville 3-4 (incomplete metabolic response) are escalated to more intensive regimens (escalated BEACOPP) or receive additional radiation. This approach has reduced treatment toxicity while maintaining efficacy.
alternativeTreatments.effectiveness: EORTC H10F showed that interim PET-negative patients can safely omit radiation (or receive reduced chemotherapy) without compromising outcome. Deauville 3 patients (partial response) may continue standard therapy with good outcomes. Deauville 4-5 (significant residual activity) requires treatment escalation.

alternativeTreatments.name: Reduced chemotherapy for favorable early-stage HL
alternativeTreatments.description: For early-stage favorable HL (stage I-II without risk factors), 2 cycles of ABVD + 20 Gy ISRT achieves >95% cure rates. Trials are investigating whether radiation can be omitted in selected favorable patients with complete metabolic response on interim PET, reducing radiation-related late effects (secondary malignancies, cardiovascular disease).
alternativeTreatments.effectiveness: 2 cycles ABVD + 20 Gy ISRT produces long-term disease control in >95% of early favorable HL. The ongoing debate is whether certain favorable subsets can avoid radiation entirely with PET-guided de-escalation.

whySeeSpecialist: A hematologist/oncologist specializing in Hodgkin lymphoma should be involved from diagnosis through treatment and follow-up. HL has unique management considerations: it is one of the most curable malignancies but has significant late effects of treatment (secondary cancers, cardiovascular disease, pulmonary fibrosis, infertility), making it critical to tailor treatment intensity to risk group. Response-adapted therapy requires specialist interpretation of interim PET findings. The management of relapsed/refractory disease (brentuximab, checkpoint inhibitors, salvage chemotherapy, transplant) requires experienced centers. Follow-up surveillance for recurrence and late effects requires longitudinal specialist care.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a hematologist/oncologist at a center with a dedicated lymphoma program and experience with HL. Ask about: experience with response-adapted therapy (interim PET interpretation), availability of all chemotherapy regimens (ABVD, escalated BEACOPP, BV-AVD), brentuximab vedotin and checkpoint inhibitor therapy, clinical trials including novel agents and bispecific antibodies, autologous and allogeneic transplant capability for relapsed disease, survivorship clinic for late effects monitoring. Academic cancer centers and NCI-designated centers have the most experience.
preventionStrategies: No established preventive strategies for HL — the disease arises from EBV infection and stochastic genomic events in germinal center B-cells that cannot be prevented
Avoid smoking — reduces risk of bleomycin pulmonary toxicity in patients receiving ABVD, and reduces long-term cardiovascular risk
HPV vaccination — reduces risk of subsequent cancers that share risk factors with HL (though no direct HL prevention benefit)
lifestyleModifications: Smoking cessation before ABVD chemotherapy — smoking increases bleomycin pulmonary toxicity risk significantly; cessation should be achieved before starting treatment
Infection prevention during chemotherapy — hand washing, food safety, avoiding crowds during neutropenic periods
Physical activity during and after treatment — maintains cardiovascular health, reduces fatigue, improves quality of life; exercise is safe during chemotherapy and improves treatment tolerance

Fertility preservation — discuss sperm banking, egg/embryo freezing, and ovarian tissue cryopreservation before starting chemotherapy; alkylating agents (cyclophosphamide in BEACOPP) carry high infertility risk; ABVD has lower but not zero risk
Long-term cardiovascular monitoring — regular blood pressure, lipid panels, and cardiac imaging given doxorubicin cardiotoxicity risk; annual echocardiogram for 10+ years after treatment

recommended: High-protein diet during chemotherapy — supports immune function and counteracts protein catabolism: lean meats, fish, eggs, legumes, dairy
Anti-inflammatory foods — fruits, vegetables, omega-3 fatty acids (fatty fish) — may support immune recovery and reduce treatment-related inflammation
Hydration — adequate fluid intake during chemotherapy, particularly with doxorubicin to reduce cardiotoxicity risk and maintain renal function
Small frequent meals during periods of nausea — frequent, bland, smaller meals are better tolerated than large meals
Ginger or mint for chemotherapy-related nausea — natural antiemetic options; discuss with oncology team before using supplements
avoid: Grapefruit and pomegranate juice — contain CYP3A4 inhibitors that may interfere with doxorubicin, brentuximab, and other chemotherapy metabolisms; discuss with pharmacist
Excessive alcohol — worsens doxorubicin cardiotoxicity and immunosuppression; limit to social drinking
Raw or undercooked foods during neutropenic periods — risk of foodborne infection; cook all meat, eggs, and seafood thoroughly
High-dose antioxidant supplements (vitamin C, E, beta-carotene) during chemotherapy — may interfere with oxidative mechanisms of chemotherapy agents; discuss with oncology team
Grapefruit and St. John's Wort — both affect CYP3A4 and can interfere with chemotherapy and supportive care medications

exerciseGuidelines: Exercise during and after HL treatment is beneficial and safe. During ABVD chemotherapy, moderate aerobic exercise (walking 30 minutes most days) is recommended to combat fatigue, maintain cardiovascular function, and improve quality of life. Avoid strenuous exercise during neutropenic periods (ANC <0.5 x 10^9/L). After completing treatment, a gradual return to regular exercise is encouraged — survivorship exercise programs improve fatigue, cardiovascular health, and body image. Long-term survivors should avoid very high-intensity exercise if they have residual cardiac, pulmonary, or orthopedic limitations from treatment.
dailyManagement: Report fever >38 degrees Celsius immediately during chemotherapy — febrile neutropenia is a medical emergency requiring same-day evaluation
Track blood counts — understand when counts are expected to be at nadir (typically days 10-14 of ABVD cycle) and plan activities accordingly

Monitor for peripheral neuropathy — early signs of neuropathy from vincristine (numbness, tingling in fingers/toes) should be reported early; dose modification or cessation prevents irreversible neuropathy
Breathlessness or cough during or after ABVD — should prompt evaluation for bleomycin pulmonary toxicity; pulmonary function testing and chest CT are indicated
Skin changes during radiation — skin in the radiation field will redden and may peel; gentle skin care with recommended topical agents is important
Regular follow-up imaging — PET-CT and CT scans at regular intervals to monitor for recurrence; adherence to surveillance schedule is critical
prognosis: HL is one of the most curable malignancies in adults, with modern first-line therapy achieving 5-year overall survival of 85-90% and 5-year progression-free survival of 80-85% across all risk groups. Early-stage favorable HL: cure rates >95% with ABVD x 2-3 cycles + ISRT. Early-stage unfavorable HL: 5-year PFS 85-90% with ABVD x 4 + ISRT or escalated BEACOPP. Advanced-stage HL: 5-year PFS 75-85% with ABVD x 6-8, escalated BEACOPP, or BV-AVD. Adverse prognostic factors include: advanced stage (III-IV), B symptoms, bulky mediastinal mass (>10 cm or >1/3 thoracic diameter), elevated LDH, >2 involved nodal sites, and extranodal disease. Interim PET-negativity after 2 cycles of ABVD is the single most powerful predictor of excellent outcome (>90% 3-year PFS). Relapsed/refractory HL still has meaningful options: salvage chemotherapy + autologous transplant cures 30-50% of relapsed chemosensitive disease; brentuximab and checkpoint inhibitors provide durable responses in transplant-ineligible or post-transplant patients.
recoveryTimeline: With ABVD x 2-3 cycles + ISRT (early favorable): treatment completes in 2-4 months; fatigue persists for 1-3 months post-treatment; full energy recovery over 3-6 months; late effects monitoring begins. With ABVD x 4-6 cycles + ISRT (early unfavorable/advanced): treatment completes in 4-6 months; cumulative fatigue and myelosuppression accumulate; recovery of energy over 3-6 months post-treatment. With escalated BEACOPP x 6 (advanced high-risk): most intensive regimen; hospitalization for neutropenic fevers likely; fertility risk significant; recovery over 6-12 months. With BV-AVD x 6: similar timeline to ABVD but without bleomycin pulmonary toxicity; neuropathy from brentuximab requires monitoring.
complications: Secondary malignancies — the most serious late effect of HL treatment. Radiation increases risk of breast, lung, thyroid, and GI cancers; alkylating agent chemotherapy (BEACOPP) increases risk of AML and MDS. Cumulative risk of secondary cancer is 10-20% at 30 years. Annual mammography and surveillance for secondary cancers is essential.

Cardiovascular disease — doxorubicin causes dose-dependent cardiomyopathy (congestive heart failure risk with >250 mg/m^2 cumulative dose); radiation to mediastinum increases coronary artery disease risk (particularly with doses >30 Gy), pericardial disease, and valvular disease. Annual cardiac monitoring is standard.

Pulmonary fibrosis and bleomycin pulmonary toxicity — bleomycin can cause interstitial pneumonitis and pulmonary fibrosis; risk increases with cumulative dose, older age, smoking, and supplemental oxygen use. Annual pulmonary function monitoring is recommended.

Infertility — cyclophosphamide in escalated BEACOPP causes gonadal toxicity and high risk of premature ovarian failure (women) and azoospermia (men). Sperm banking and ovarian tissue/egg preservation should be discussed before treatment. ABVD has significantly lower fertility risk.
Hypothyroidism — radiation to the neck (cervical/supraclavicular nodes) damages the thyroid gland; risk of hypothyroidism is 20-30% by 20 years post-radiation. Annual TSH monitoring is required.
Fatigue — persistent cancer-related fatigue for months to years after completion of treatment, affecting quality of life and work capacity; requires multidisciplinary management.

Secondary leukemia and MDS — alkylating agents (particularly cyclophosphamide in BEACOPP) carry a dose-dependent risk of secondary AML and MDS; risk is 1-3% at 20 years with ABVD; higher with escalated BEACOPP.

supportResources.name: Lymphoma Research Foundation (LRF)
supportResources.description: Comprehensive patient education, support services, clinical trial matching, and advocacy for all lymphoma types including HL. Offers a helpline, patient education materials, and support groups.

supportResources.name: Leukemia & Lymphoma Society (LLS)
supportResources.description: Blood cancer information including HL resources, support services, financial assistance, and clinical trial matching.

supportResources.name: CureSearch for Children's Cancer
supportResources.description: Resources for pediatric and young adult HL patients, including survivorship information for those treated in childhood/adolescence.

supportResources.name: NCCN Guidelines for Hodgkin Lymphoma
supportResources.description: Freely accessible clinical practice guideline for HL — the standard reference for evidence-based management.

confusedWithConditions.name: Non-Hodgkin Lymphoma (NHL)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: NHL is a heterogeneous group of lymphomas (excluding HL) arising from B-cells (most common), T-cells, or NK-cells. While both are lymphomas, HL has a unique pathology (Reed-Sternberg cells in an inflammatory background) and distinct immunophenotype (CD30+, CD15+, CD45-). HL treatment (ABVD-based) differs from most NHL treatment (CHOP-based). Some aggressive NHLs (primary mediastinal large B-cell lymphoma, grey zone lymphoma) share features with HL.

confusedWithConditions.name: Infectious Mononucleosis (EBV infection)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: EBV-positive infectious mononucleosis causes cervical lymphadenopathy, fatigue, and fever that can clinically mimic HL. The key distinction is the presence of Reed-Sternberg cells on biopsy in HL, and EBV-driven atypical lymphocytosis without dysplasia. EBV mono resolves; HL persists and progresses without treatment.

confusedWithConditions.name: Sarcoidosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Sarcoidosis causes mediastinal and cervical lymphadenopathy that can mimic HL, particularly on imaging. Non-caseating granulomas on biopsy differentiate sarcoidosis (which can mimic HL clinically) from HL (which has Reed-Sternberg cells). Both can have EBV association. The biopsy findings are definitive.

confusedWithConditions.name: Diffuse Large B-Cell Lymphoma (DLBCL)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: DLBCL is the most common aggressive NHL, presenting with rapidly enlarging lymph nodes, often B symptoms. While both are aggressive lymphomas, DLBCL has sheets of large B-cells (CD20+, CD45+) without Reed-Sternberg cells. DLBCL is managed with R-CHOP; HL is managed with ABVD. Biopsy distinguishes them definitively.

relatedConditions.name: Non-Hodgkin Lymphoma
relatedConditions.relevance: A heterogeneous group of lymphomas sharing lymph node origin with HL but differing in pathology, immunophenotype, and treatment. Some NHL subtypes (primary mediastinal large B-cell lymphoma) share features with HL and may be managed with HL-inspired regimens.

relatedConditions.name: Primary Mediastinal Large B-Cell Lymphoma
relatedConditions.relevance: An aggressive B-cell lymphoma arising in the mediastinum with clinicopathological features overlapping with classical HL (CD30+, large mediastinal mass). May be managed with DA-EPOCH-R or HL-inspired regimens. Requires careful pathological distinction from HL.

relatedConditions.name: Grey Zone Lymphoma
relatedConditions.relevance: A rare B-cell lymphoma with features intermediate between classical HL and primary mediastinal large B-cell lymphoma — combining CD30, CD15, and CD20 positivity with aggressive clinical behavior. Requires specialized treatment protocols.

relatedConditions.name: Nodular Lymphocyte-Predominant Hodgkin Lymphoma (NLPHL)
relatedConditions.relevance: A distinct entity from classical HL with different biology (CD20+, CD30-) and management approach (rituximab-based). Has an indolent course with waxing/waning lymphadenopathy; transformation to aggressive NHL occurs in 5-10%.

faqs.question: Is Hodgkin lymphoma curable?
faqs.answer: Yes — Hodgkin lymphoma is one of the most curable malignancies in adults. Modern first-line treatment achieves long-term disease control (5-year progression-free survival) in 85-90% of patients across all risk groups. Early-stage favorable HL has cure rates exceeding 95%. Even advanced-stage HL has 75-85% 5-year PFS with modern chemotherapy and targeted agents. For the minority who experience relapse, salvage chemotherapy and autologous transplant can still cure a significant proportion.

faqs.question: What are the stages of Hodgkin lymphoma?
faqs.answer: HL is staged using the Ann Arbor/Lugano classification: Stage I — involvement of a single lymph node region or one extralymphatic site. Stage II — involvement of two or more lymph node regions on the same side of the diaphragm. Stage III — lymph node regions on both sides of the diaphragm. Stage IV — disseminated (diffuse) involvement of one or more extralymphatic organs (liver, bone marrow, lung). 'A' indicates no B symptoms; 'B' indicates presence of B symptoms (fever, night sweats, weight loss >10%). Bulky disease (mass >10 cm or >1/3 thoracic diameter) is designated as 'X'.

faqs.question: What is the standard treatment for early-stage Hodgkin lymphoma?
faqs.answer: Early-stage favorable HL (stage I-II without risk factors): 2-3 cycles of ABVD chemotherapy + 20-30 Gy involved-site radiation therapy (ISRT) achieves >95% cure rates. Early-stage unfavorable HL (stage I-II with risk factors — B symptoms, bulky disease, >2 nodal sites, elevated ESR): 4 cycles ABVD + 30 Gy ISRT, or response-adapted approach (interim PET-negative patients may receive reduced chemotherapy). The goal is to maximize cure while minimizing late effects.

faqs.question: What is the treatment for advanced-stage Hodgkin lymphoma?
faqs.answer: Advanced-stage HL (stage III-IV or any stage with B symptoms) is treated with 6 cycles of ABVD, or escalated BEACOPP (for high-risk patients), or BV-AVD (brentuximab vedotin + doxorubicin, vinblastine, dacarbazine — now standard for many patients). Interim PET after 2 cycles guides response-adapted therapy: PET-negative patients continue; PET-positive patients may be escalated to escalated BEACOPP. Brentuximab vedotin combinations are increasingly used first-line.

faqs.question: What is interim PET in Hodgkin lymphoma?
faqs.answer: Interim PET-CT is performed after 2 cycles of ABVD chemotherapy to assess early metabolic response. The Deauville 5-point scale scores FDG uptake: 1 = no residual uptake; 2 = uptake less than or equal to liver; 3 = uptake between liver and mediastinum; 4 = uptake moderately higher than liver; 5 = uptake markedly higher than liver or new lesions. Deauville 1-2 indicates complete metabolic response (excellent prognosis); Deauville 4-5 indicates residual disease requiring treatment escalation.

faqs.question: What are the long-term effects of Hodgkin lymphoma treatment?
faqs.answer: HL survivors carry significant late effects from treatment: secondary cancers (breast, lung, thyroid, AML/MDS) — risk 10-20% at 30 years, higher with radiation and alkylating agents; cardiovascular disease — cardiomyopathy from doxorubicin, coronary artery disease from mediastinal radiation; pulmonary fibrosis from bleomycin; infertility from cyclophosphamide in BEACOPP; hypothyroidism from neck radiation; fatigue. Lifelong surveillance is essential.

faqs.question: Is Hodgkin lymphoma hereditary?
faqs.answer: HL is not directly hereditary, but familial clustering occurs — siblings of HL patients have a 3-5 fold increased risk. Inherited HLA variants (particularly HLA-A*01 and HLA-DPB1*0201) contribute to this familial risk. Most patients have no family history. EBV infection history is a stronger epidemiological risk factor than family history.

faqs.question: What is nodular lymphocyte-predominant Hodgkin lymphoma (NLPHL)?
faqs.answer: NLPHL is a distinct entity from classical HL (5% of HL cases) with different biology and management. The malignant cells (L&H or 'popcorn' cells) express CD20 and CD45 (not CD30 or CD15) and are surrounded by rosetting T-cells. NLPHL tends to be indolent, presenting as early-stage disease with waxing/waning lymphadenopathy. It is managed with rituximab-based approaches (rituximab +/- limited chemotherapy) rather than ABVD. Transformation to aggressive B-cell NHL occurs in 5-10% of cases.

faqs.question: What is Brentuximab vedotin and when is it used?
faqs.answer: Brentuximab vedotin is an antibody-drug conjugate (ADC) linking an anti-CD30 antibody to monomethyl auristatin E (MMAE), a microtubule inhibitor. It is used in: (1) relapsed/refractory cHL — after failure of autologous transplant or after at least 2 prior lines of therapy; (2) post-transplant consolidation — for patients at high risk of relapse (AETHERA trial); (3) first-line advanced-stage — combined with AVD (BV-AVD) as an alternative to ABVD (ECHELON-1 trial), showing superior outcomes.

faqs.question: How is relapsed Hodgkin lymphoma treated?
faqs.answer: Relapsed HL is managed with salvage chemotherapy (ICE, DHAP, or brentuximab-based regimens) to achieve chemosensitivity, followed by high-dose chemotherapy (BEAM or CVB conditioning) and autologous stem cell transplant — this cures 30-50% of patients with chemosensitive relapse. For patients who are transplant-ineligible or who relapse post-transplant, brentuximab vedotin, nivolumab, pembrolizumab, or gemcitabine-based regimens provide meaningful responses. Allogeneic transplant is considered for selected patients with suitable donors.

metaTitle: Hodgkin Lymphoma: Symptoms, Treatment, Stages & Prognosis | aihealz
metaDescription: Hodgkin lymphoma: causes, stages, ABVD chemotherapy, brentuximab vedotin, radiation therapy, interim PET response assessment, late effects, and prognosis. Reviewed by oncology specialists.

images.altText: Medical illustration of Hodgkin lymphoma showing Reed-Sternberg cells in lymph node tissue
images.caption: Hodgkin lymphoma is characterized by Reed-Sternberg cells — large binucleated tumor cells — in a reactive inflammatory background.

images.altText: Microscopic image of Reed-Sternberg cell characteristic of Hodgkin lymphoma
images.caption: Reed-Sternberg cells are pathognomonic for Hodgkin lymphoma — large cells with bilobed nuclei ('owl-eye' appearance) and prominent nucleoli.

images.altText: Diagram of lymphatic system showing lymph node regions for Hodgkin lymphoma staging
images.caption: Hodgkin lymphoma staging follows the Ann Arbor classification based on lymph node region involvement.
